food_name: Ayam Geprek Spicy Level 5 (Fiery Devil Chilli)
aliases: Smashed Fried Chicken with Fiery Chilli Sambal

english: Smashed Fried Chicken with Fiery Chilli Sambal

food_subgroup: Fried chicken with sambal / chilli paste

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Ayam Geprek. FOODON_03311434 (fried chicken food product) is used as the closest parent. The sambal/chilli component is not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. Ayam Geprek is a smashed fried chicken dish with sambal; no dedicated FoodEx2 term exists for this preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Ayam Geprek or Indonesian smashed fried chicken with sambal.

allergens: possible soy (if soy sauce used in marinade)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Ayam Geprek is approximately 250 g, comprising one piece of battered deep-fried chicken (roughly 180–200 g) smashed and topped with a generous portion of fiery birdseye chilli sambal (approximately 50 g).

nutrient_source.title: Ayam Goreng (Fried Chicken) — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken (breaded) and chilli sambal component estimation from ASEAN Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Ayam Geprek exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA fried chicken (ayam goreng) data and USDA SR Legacy breaded fried chicken (FDC ID 171477), weighted to reflect the sambal component (~20% of serving weight). The high-spice sambal contributes minimal macronutrients but adds sodium and capsaicin. Values represent a composite estimate for the full dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried chicken and mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate dishes — analogous estimate based on breaded fried chicken GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Geprek. GI is estimated at 48 (low) based on breaded fried chicken analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 38–54 for similar fried chicken preparations). The high protein and fat content of the dish, combined with the low carbohydrate fraction from the batter, supports a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (48 × 9.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 11.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100 g; ~1200 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Ayam Geprek due to its high sodium content from the sambal, marinade, and frying process; a single serving may provide approximately 50–60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from batter coating; low GI mitigates acute glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ayam Geprek has a low estimated glycaemic index (~48) due to its high protein and fat content, but the battered coating contributes moderate carbohydrates (~9.8 g/100 g); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with non-starchy vegetables to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-frying and skin; high sodium; moderate cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process and chicken skin contribute saturated fat (~3.4 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~72 mg/100 g), and the high sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) poses a risk for blood pressure elevation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in controlled portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek Spicy Level 5 (Fiery Devil Chilli) is an Indonesian fried chicken dish characterised by its intensely spicy sambal topping. 'Geprek' means 'smashed' in Javanese — the crispy battered fried chicken is pressed or smashed against a mortar-ground sambal made from birdseye chillies (cabe rawit), garlic, shallots, and salt. Level 5 denotes the highest spice intensity tier, using a fiery devil chilli (cabai setan) concoction. The dish originated in Yogyakarta, Indonesia, and became a viral street food trend across Southeast Asia in the 2010s. The chicken is first marinated, coated in a seasoned flour batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then smashed into the freshly pounded sambal. The result is a juicy, crispy chicken piece saturated with fiery, aromatic chilli paste. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~229 kcal/100 g) with a high protein content (~18.5 g/100 g) from the chicken, moderate fat from deep-frying (~13.2 g/100 g), and a relatively low carbohydrate fraction (~9.8 g/100 g) from the batter. Sodium is elevated (~480 mg/100 g) due to the sambal and seasoning, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The estimated glycaemic index is low (~48), suitable for moderate consumption by individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is typically halal and dairy-free but contains gluten from the batter. It is commonly served with steamed rice and is a popular lunch and dinner option in Indonesian restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How spicy is ayam geprek level 5?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Ayam Geprek exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA). Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA fried chicken (ayam goreng) and USDA SR Legacy breaded fried chicken data, weighted for the sambal component. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable fried chicken preparations. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish is compositionally similar to well-documented fried chicken entries and the adaptation methodology is transparent.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA ayam goreng data and USDA SR Legacy breaded fried chicken (FDC ID 171477); no direct laboratory analysis of Ayam Geprek Spicy Lv.5 was available in any approved source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this adaptation. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for fried chicken. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (48, low) based on breaded fried chicken data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Ayam Geprek exists in the approved GI literature. 5. The 'Fiery Devil Chilli' descriptor and 'Spicy Level 5' designation are menu-specific marketing terms from Smile Martabak - Funan (field survey data); they do not alter the fundamental nutritional composition of the dish. 6. Sodium content may vary significantly between restaurants depending on sambal recipe and marinade; the value of 480 mg/100 g represents a mid-range estimate. 7. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON ('Stuffed Pancake', 'Pancake', 'Coffee') appear to be restaurant-level tags for Smile Martabak and are not relevant to the food item itself; they have been disregarded. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous entries.
